title: Signal Drums
desc: Across flats this open, a low drum note can carry farther than sight.

steps.text:
Rao'Pa Sahkk is so broad and level that sound travels across it with unnerving clarity at dawn and dusk. Mipedian crossing crews make use of that fact with low signal drums set along the safer routes. One slow pattern means hold position. Two quick beats means drift left. A rising roll means weather or heat trouble farther ahead.

Last night's dry wind loosened two hides and buried part of an echo board line under sand. The drum keeper arrives with a leather cord bundle, a tuning peg, and the practical expression of someone responsible for messages other creatures may hear miles away. "Bad drum work out here turns into good panic somewhere else," she says.

The nearest station needs its hide retensioned, or the buried echo boards need to be cleared and reset so the note keeps traveling at the right height over the flats.

options.label: Clear and reset the buried echo boards

steps.text:
The drum itself is only waist high, stretched over a shallow clay basin sunk into the sand for stability. You work the laces in small turns while the keeper taps the hide with two curved sticks and listens for the note to tighten from dull thud to carrying tone.

The change is subtle up close and dramatic at distance. On the first good strike, the sound runs outward over the flats instead of dying beside the station. There is no dune or wall to catch it, only open false sea and the cool hour before full light. Far off, another station answers with the same pattern a breath later.

The keeper allows herself a brief grin. "There. Now if someone gets lost, the drum will have the decency to sound confident." She marks the station on her slate, then taps the hide once more just to hear the note go traveling.

The echo boards are thin angled plates set low in the sand beyond each drum line. Their job is not to make the signal louder, only to keep it skimming across the flats instead of bleeding upward into empty air. When a sand drift buries them, the next station hears only half the message.

You dig carefully with a flat bone shovel, uncovering each board, brushing grit from the face, and resetting the angle with the keeper's level rod. Once the line is restored, she strikes the drum behind you and the note slides past your shoulder like a physical thing, low and clean and purposeful.

For a moment the whole desert seems arranged around that one traveling sound. Flat ground, pale sky, hidden heat, and a note moving over them all to tell distant creatures that the lane still holds. It is a stern kind of beauty, but beauty all the same.

options.label: Pack the shovel and log the station
